messages.content: lets talk about phase 2 and making the template system and pack system more robust

Yes. Phase 2 is where GROVE stops being “packs render files” and becomes a real composition system.

Right now, your notes point to two missing capabilities:

packs can render whole files, but cannot inject into shared files like the base .grove/GROVE.md; there is no anchored merge mechanism yet 

phase2_chat

you already have the idea of layered loading and scoped rules, but not a first-class machine-readable discovery layer for “if you need X, look here” guidance 

phase2_chat

 

README

That lines up cleanly with the WISC-style model you’ve been circling: keep the always-loaded layer lean, move specific material into scoped rules, and keep heavyweight docs discoverable on demand instead of stuffed into the base context 

README

 

cole_transcript

My recommendation is that Phase 2 should focus on four upgrades.

1. Add anchored injection as a first-class pack contribution

This is the biggest missing piece.

Today, packs contribute whole files. Phase 2 should let a pack say:

inject this snippet

into this target file

at this named anchor

with deterministic ordering

under managed markers so sync can safely re-render only the generated region

That gives you extensibility without turning shared files into hand-edited chaos.

A good shape would be something like:

TOML
[[contributes.injections]]
target = ".grove/GROVE.md"
anchor = "grove:rules"
source = "snippets/python-rules.md"
order = 200
id = "python-rules"

And the target file would have stable anchor markers like:

Markdown
<!-- grove:anchor:grove:rules:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:grove:rules:end -->

Then sync renders:

Markdown
<!-- grove:managed:injection python-rules:start -->
...rendered content...
<!-- grove:managed:injection python-rules:end -->

That buys you a lot:

base pack owns the skeleton

feature packs extend it

user edits outside managed regions survive

re-sync is deterministic

2. Separate “template rendering” from “document composition”

This is the architectural step that will keep the system from getting muddy.

Right now the mental model is mostly “template -> file.”
Phase 2 should split it into:

render stage: resolve variables, conditions, snippets

compose stage: merge rendered artifacts into destination files

sync stage: write/update only managed regions

That distinction matters because once injections exist, a file may be assembled from:

a base template

multiple pack snippets

generated discovery sections

maybe future analysis-derived content

So I’d treat files as a composed output graph, not just direct template products.

In other words:

templates produce content fragments

contributions declare where fragments belong

composer builds the final file

That makes the pack system much easier to grow later.

3. Add a discovery index as structured data, not just prose

This is the second big Phase 2 deliverable.

You already have the concept:

always-loaded base guidance

path-scoped rules

heavier reference docs used on demand 

phase2_chat

 

README

What’s missing is the machine-readable registry that says what is discoverable and when.

I would add a generated file like:

TOML
# .grove/discovery.toml

[[topics]]
id = "python-testing"
title = "Python testing"
when_paths = ["tests/**", "**/test_*.py"]
summary = "Testing conventions, fixtures, and commands."

[[topics.pointers]]
path = ".grove/rules/python-testing.md"
why = "Use for testing conventions and fixture patterns."

[[topics.commands]]
name = "uv run pytest"
why = "Run the Python test suite."

And maybe a human-facing rendered companion:

.grove/DISCOVERY.md

That gives you both machine and human value.

This is important because it turns “just-in-time discoverability” into a real subsystem instead of a loose documentation habit.

4. Add pack dependency and capability metadata

Once packs can inject and expose discovery topics, you need stronger metadata so they compose predictably.

I’d add things like:

TOML
[pack]
name = "python"
version = "0.2.0"

[capabilities]
provides = ["language:python", "testing:pytest"]
depends_on = ["base"]
conflicts_with = []

And for each contribution:

TOML
[[contributes.rules]]
id = "python-core-rules"
paths = ["**/*.py"]
priority = 100

That lets the engine answer questions like:

which packs are active?

which one owns this rule?

what happens if two packs inject into the same anchor?

what order should contributions be applied?

Without this, Phase 2 will get brittle fast.

What I would make the actual Phase 2 scope

I would define Phase 2 like this:

Phase 2A — Robust composition

introduce contributes.injections

introduce anchor markers

implement managed-region sync

deterministic ordering and conflict detection

Phase 2B — Structured discovery

introduce discovery.toml generation

allow packs to contribute discovery topics/pointers/commands

render optional .grove/DISCOVERY.md

Phase 2C — Stronger pack metadata

pack ids, versions, dependencies, capabilities

contribution ids and priorities

collision validation

Phase 2D — Validation and observability

dry-run compose

diff preview

“why is this here?” provenance output

lint for anchors, duplicate ids, unresolved references

That last one matters a lot. You want to be able to ask the system:

why is this block in GROVE.md?

which pack inserted it?

which anchor received it?

what source template produced it?

If you don’t have provenance, debugging the pack system will get ugly.

The design principle I’d use

My strongest opinion here:

Do not make packs directly edit arbitrary files.
Make them contribute structured content into explicitly declared managed regions.

That keeps GROVE disciplined.

So instead of:

“pack can patch any file with regex”

do:

“pack can inject into known anchors”

“pack can contribute rules”

“pack can contribute discovery topics”

“pack can contribute reference docs”

That is much safer and much easier to reason about.

The file model I’d aim for

I’d make the system revolve around these output classes:

base docs
always-loaded, composed, anchor-enabled
example: .grove/GROVE.md

scoped rules
path-triggered, pack-owned
example: .grove/rules/python.md

reference docs
heavyweight, not auto-loaded
example: .grove/docs/workflows.md

discovery index
machine-readable routing layer
example: .grove/discovery.toml

human index
rendered summary for humans and agents
example: .grove/DISCOVERY.md

That structure fits the layered loading model already described in your notes and the WISC material 

phase2_chat

 

README

The biggest trap to avoid

The trap is trying to make Phase 2 too magical.

Don’t build:

arbitrary AST mutation

freeform patching

packs that rewrite user docs everywhere

“smart” merge behavior with hidden rules

Do build:

explicit anchors

explicit managed blocks

explicit discovery records

explicit precedence and ownership

Boring here is good.

My blunt recommendation

If I were sequencing this, I would make the next implementation target:

contributes.injections

anchor parser/composer

managed block sync

discovery registry generation

provenance + validation CLI

That’s the clean spine.

Once that exists, later features become much easier:

conditional injections

project analysis emitting discovery hints

pack recommendations

skill/tool surfacing

language/framework auto-activation

So yes, Phase 2 should absolutely be about making the template and pack system more robust — but specifically by turning it into a composable, validated document graph instead of a simple template copier.

If you want, I’ll turn this into a concrete Phase 2 spec with proposed manifest schema, file layout, sync rules, and validation behavior.

questions...

- should we continue calling it GROVE.md as a branded file? i like it quite a bit. im wondering if most tools use something like RULES.md.
- i love your idea about anchors with start and end in the files! in the toml example, would contributes.injections have multiple entries so we can inject into multiple files? if not should we have it something like [[contributes.injections.GROVE.md]] so the target is in the name? i would rather not do it like that but we can if needed.
- when sync renders, i feel like we should not have the markers rendered, just the content. am i off? if it resyncs or rerenders, it would use the base anyway, right? or are we planning on letting a user directly edit the grove files once rendered? we probably need an elegant way to do user edits
- i didnt really understand how the discovery system works. elaborate.

ideas...
- we need a system that installs commands and skills for other tools that point at grove's system such as cursor, claude code, codex and the like
- we need to strip the base pack down to minimal. that way other packs can inject into it but not have a super opinionated base

I think you’re asking exactly the right Phase 2 questions.

On the big picture, I’d steer GROVE toward two principles that fit the material you already have:

keep the always-loaded layer extremely lean, because the whole point of the tiered model is that global rules stay small while scoped rules and deep docs load later only when relevant 

README

 

cole_transcript

make extensions explicit and structured, because today your pack model already has whole-file templates and path-scoped rules, but not anchored injection or a first-class discovery index yet 

phase2_chat

1) Should it stay GROVE.md or become something like RULES.md?

My vote: keep GROVE.md.

You already have a strong conceptual model where .grove/GROVE.md is the always-loaded base document, while pack-specific guidance lives in scoped rule files like .grove/rules/python.md 

phase2_chat

 

phase2_chat

. That is actually cleaner than overloading everything into RULES.md.

What I would do is treat:

.grove/GROVE.md = branded, canonical Tier 1 entrypoint

.grove/rules/*.md = scoped Tier 2 rules

.grove/docs/*.md = Tier 3 reference docs

.grove/discovery.toml = machine-readable routing layer

That keeps the brand and the architecture aligned.

The only reason to rename it to RULES.md would be compatibility with tools that hardcode filenames. But I would not sacrifice the GROVE identity for that. Instead, for tools that expect a conventional file, I’d generate or install a thin compatibility shim, like:

CLAUDE.md

AGENTS.md

RULES.md

maybe tool-specific command/skills wrappers

Those shims can simply say “the canonical system instructions live in .grove/GROVE.md” and either mirror or reference the relevant sections.

So: brand stays GROVE.md; compatibility happens through adapters.

2) Should [[contributes.injections]] support multiple entries?

Yes, absolutely.

That TOML array-of-tables form is already the cleanest way to support multiple injections into multiple targets. You do not need something like [[contributes.injections.GROVE.md]].

I would keep it like this:

TOML
[[contributes.injections]]
id = "python-grove-guidance"
target = ".grove/GROVE.md"
anchor = "grove:guidance"
source = "snippets/python-guidance.md"
order = 200

[[contributes.injections]]
id = "python-cursor-command"
target = ".grove/adapters/cursor.md"
anchor = "grove:commands"
source = "snippets/cursor-command.md"
order = 100

That buys you:

many injections per pack

many targets per pack

multiple packs contributing to the same target

simple validation for duplicate ids, missing anchors, and ordering collisions

If you put the target in the TOML table name, you make the schema awkward and harder to validate. The current pattern is more extensible.

So yes: multiple [[contributes.injections]] entries is the right design.

3) Should rendered files contain managed markers, or only final content?

This is the subtle one.

I think you do need markers, but they should be elegant and minimal.

The reason is that if users are allowed to edit rendered GROVE files directly—and I think they should be allowed in carefully chosen regions—then the sync engine needs a reliable way to know:

which content is system-managed

which pack owns it

where it starts and ends

what must be replaced on re-sync

what must be left alone

Without markers, re-sync becomes guesswork.

The earlier design note already pointed in this direction: managed block markers let sync replace only the injected block and preserve user-edited content around it 

phase2_chat

 

phase2_chat

.

So I would split this into three levels of markers:

A. Anchor markers in the base template

These belong in the base-authored file and define legal insertion zones.

Markdown
<!-- grove:anchor:guidance:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:guidance:end -->
B. Managed block markers around injected content

These are rendered by sync and should remain in the file.

Markdown
<!-- grove:managed:python-grove-guidance:start -->
... injected content ...
<!-- grove:managed:python-grove-guidance:end -->
C. Explicit user-editable regions

These give the human a sanctioned place to customize.

Markdown
<!-- grove:user:notes:start -->
<!-- grove:user:notes:end -->

That gives you a really nice contract:

system can safely replace managed blocks

system never touches user regions

users can edit outside managed blocks if you want to allow that, but the cleanest rule is: edits should live in user regions or overlays

My recommendation is: yes, render markers. Not because they’re pretty, but because they make re-sync deterministic and safe.

If you want them less visible, you can keep them as compact HTML comments. That’s usually unobtrusive enough.

4) How should user edits work elegantly?

I would not rely on “just edit wherever and hope sync is smart.” That gets messy fast.

I’d use one of these two models:

Preferred model: user overlay regions

Each important generated file can include explicit user sections.

Example:

Markdown
# GROVE

... managed intro ...

## Project-specific notes
<!-- grove:user:project-notes:start -->
<!-- grove:user:project-notes:end -->

Sync preserves those regions exactly.

Stronger long-term model: separate overlay files

Instead of letting users edit the generated file much, let them edit parallel files like:

.grove/local/user-notes.md

.grove/local/overrides.toml

Then GROVE composes those into the final document at render time.

That is even cleaner, but it’s a little less friendly at first.

My vote for Phase 2:

support managed markers

support user regions

optionally add overlay files later

5) Discovery system — how it actually works

This is the part I think needs the most concretizing.

Right now, you already have the concept of progressive disclosure:

Tier 1 always-loaded base rules

Tier 2 path-triggered scoped rules

Tier 3 heavyweight reference docs that should not be loaded unless relevant 

README

 

README

And the transcript also adds another useful layer: tiny “descriptions” up front, then load the full skill or deeper docs only if needed

The missing piece is a machine-readable routing map.

What discovery is

Discovery is the system that answers:

“I’m touching Python tests. What GROVE materials matter?”

“I need the canonical lint/test command for this repo. Where is it?”

“I’m in Cursor, not Claude Code. Which installed command/skill should I use?”

“I’m working in src/foo/bar.py. Which rules, docs, skills, or adapters are relevant?”

What discovery is not

It is not the content itself.

It is an index pointing to the content.

Practical shape

I’d make a file like:

TOML
# .grove/discovery.toml

[[topics]]
id = "python-testing"
title = "Python testing"
summary = "Testing conventions, commands, fixtures, and review expectations."
when_paths = ["tests/**/*.py", "**/test_*.py"]
tags = ["python", "testing"]

[[topics.pointers]]
kind = "rule"
path = ".grove/rules/python-testing.md"
why = "Use for Python test conventions."

[[topics.pointers]]
kind = "doc"
path = ".grove/docs/testing-deep-dive.md"
why = "Use when changing fixtures, integration patterns, or CI behavior."

[[topics.commands]]
name = "uv run pytest"
why = "Canonical test runner."

[[topics.tools]]
name = "cursor:grove-prime-python"
why = "Loads Python-focused context in Cursor."
How it gets used

A tool or agent can query it by:

current path

current language/framework

requested task type

current host tool (Cursor, Codex, Claude Code)

Then it returns a small answer like:

Relevant GROVE assets:

.grove/rules/python-testing.md

.grove/docs/testing-deep-dive.md

command: uv run pytest

tool adapter: cursor:grove-prime-python

So discovery is basically the routing layer for just-in-time context.

Why it matters

Without discovery, the system knows content exists, but not how to surface it systematically.

With discovery:

packs can publish what they provide

adapters can install commands and skills against known topics

tools can ask GROVE what to load instead of hardcoding their own logic

That fits perfectly with the “load context just in time, not just in case” model from WISC 

cole_transcript

6) Your idea: install commands and skills for Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, etc.

Yes. I think this should become a first-class Phase 2 or 2.5 capability.

I would call these adapters.

A pack should be able to contribute:

base GROVE docs

rules

discovery topics

tool adapters

So a Python or testing pack might install:

Cursor command templates

Claude Code slash commands or skills

Codex-compatible prompt/command files

maybe OpenClaw/OpenCode/OpenClaw-ish equivalents later

The key is: the adapter layer should not duplicate the logic. It should point back to GROVE.

So instead of every tool having its own independent rule system, the adapter should say:

here’s the command name

here’s when to use it

here’s how it queries GROVE discovery

here’s which GROVE files it loads

That keeps GROVE as the source of truth.

7) Your idea: strip the base pack down to minimal

Strong yes.

That is exactly the right move.

The WISC material is very clear that the global layer should stay lean, and anything that is not universally needed should move into scoped or on-demand material 

README

 

cole_transcript

So I’d make the base pack only responsible for:

what GROVE is

core operating philosophy

universal repo commands

universal quality bar

the tiered loading model

anchor skeletons for extension

maybe a few user-editable sections

And then let packs inject:

language-specific guidance

tool-specific compatibility notes

workflow-specific conventions

command adapters

discovery entries

So the base pack becomes infrastructure, not opinion.

That is the right architecture.

My strongest recommendations from all of this

I’d lock in these decisions:

keep .grove/GROVE.md as the canonical branded file

support multiple [[contributes.injections]] entries

keep target as a field, not in the table name

render minimal HTML comment markers for anchors and managed regions

explicitly support user-editable regions or overlays

add .grove/discovery.toml as the machine-readable routing index

add adapter contributions for Cursor / Claude Code / Codex / similar tools

aggressively slim the base pack so it is infrastructure-first

If you want, I’ll turn this into a concrete Phase 2 manifest/schema proposal with the exact TOML sections for injections, discovery, adapters, and user-preserved regions.

we will keep GROVE.md
we will keep the markers. that was a great insight
we will have specific user markers
i want to add a external tool framework for this phase 2. we will do only codex this time.
i do not want to use discovery.toml for the agent to have to use. we will render the text into the md files

Good. That tightens Phase 2 nicely.

You’ve now made four important calls:

GROVE.md stays the canonical branded file

managed markers stay

user-editable markers become first-class

external tool integration enters Phase 2, but only for Codex

discovery is rendered into markdown, not exposed as a machine-only TOML layer

I think that is the right simplification.

What this means architecturally

Phase 2 is no longer “make packs more powerful” in the abstract.

It becomes:

build a robust document composition system with safe sync, human-readable routing, and Codex integration.

That is a very clean scope.

Revised Phase 2 shape

I would frame it as five sub-systems.

1. Robust template composition

This is the anchor + injection model.

A pack can contribute:

whole files

injections into anchored files

rendered sections into shared documents

So yes, [[contributes.injections]] should absolutely allow many entries.

Example:

TOML
[[contributes.injections]]
id = "python-guidance"
target = ".grove/GROVE.md"
anchor = "grove:guidance"
source = "snippets/python-guidance.md"
order = 200

[[contributes.injections]]
id = "python-testing"
target = ".grove/rules/python-testing.md"
anchor = "grove:content"
source = "snippets/python-testing.md"
order = 100

That is better than encoding target paths into the TOML table names.

2. Managed sync with user-safe regions

This is now a core contract of GROVE.

You already decided markers stay, and I think that’s exactly right.

I would formalize three marker types:

Anchor markers

Define legal insertion zones in a base file.

Markdown
<!-- grove:anchor:guidance:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:guidance:end -->
Managed markers

Wrap generated content that GROVE owns.

Markdown
<!-- grove:managed:python-guidance:start -->
... generated content ...
<!-- grove:managed:python-guidance:end -->
User markers

Wrap regions the human owns.

Markdown
<!-- grove:user:project-notes:start -->
<!-- grove:user:project-notes:end -->

Then the sync rules are simple:

GROVE may replace managed blocks

GROVE may insert/remove managed blocks inside anchors

GROVE must preserve user blocks exactly

GROVE must not rewrite content outside managed zones unless explicitly instructed

That gives you safe re-rendering without ambiguity.

3. Human-readable routing instead of discovery.toml

This is a good constraint.

You do not want a separate machine registry that agents must learn to query. You want the routing guidance rendered directly into markdown.

That means “discovery” becomes a rendered navigation layer, not a hidden metadata API.

I would implement this as generated markdown files, probably something like:

.grove/GROVE.md for the lean global layer

.grove/INDEX.md for what exists and when to use it

.grove/rules/*.md for scoped instructions

.grove/docs/*.md for deeper references

.grove/codex/CODEX.md or similar for Codex-facing integration

So instead of this:

agent reads discovery.toml

you do this:

agent reads markdown that already contains “when to use this” guidance

Example:

Markdown
# GROVE Index

## Python
Use these when editing Python code or tests.

- `.grove/rules/python.md` — core Python coding rules
- `.grove/rules/python-testing.md` — test conventions and commands
- `.grove/docs/python-workflows.md` — deeper workflows and examples

## Codex
Use these when working through Codex integration.

- `.grove/codex/CODEX.md` — Codex adapter instructions
- `.grove/codex/commands.md` — available Codex-oriented commands

That keeps everything inspectable by humans and tools.

4. Codex external tool framework

This is a really good addition for Phase 2.

I would treat Codex support as an adapter framework, even if Codex is the only adapter right now.

That matters because the architecture should not hardcode Codex everywhere. It should hardcode the concept of an adapter, with only one implementation at first.

So Phase 2 should include something like:

TOML
[[contributes.adapters]]
tool = "codex"
type = "instruction_file"
target = ".grove/codex/CODEX.md"
source = "adapters/codex/CODEX.md"

Or maybe:

TOML
[[contributes.adapters]]
id = "codex-core"
tool = "codex"
target = ".grove/codex/CODEX.md"
source = "adapters/codex/core.md"
order = 100

Then later Cursor or Claude Code can plug into the same model without redesigning the pack schema.

What the Codex adapter should do

Not invent a separate rule system.

It should:

point Codex at GROVE.md

point Codex at the relevant rules/docs

expose canonical commands or workflows

describe how Codex should use GROVE files during planning and execution

keep the source of truth inside GROVE, not inside the adapter

So Codex is a doorway into GROVE, not a parallel universe.

5. Minimal base pack

Yes. Very strongly yes.

The base pack should become skeletal infrastructure.

I would make base own only:

the identity of GROVE

the core operating philosophy

the file structure

the marker system

the global minimum rules

anchors for pack injection

index/navigation stubs

Codex adapter anchor points if needed

It should not own lots of opinions about Python, JS, testing, research, docs, workflows, etc.

Those belong in packs.

So base becomes:

light

stable

extensible

not culturally overloaded

That is the right direction.

My recommended Phase 2 spec, updated to your choices
Phase 2A — Composition engine

Build support for:

whole-file rendering

anchor-based injection

multiple injections per pack

deterministic ordering

conflict detection

Phase 2B — Safe sync

Build support for:

managed markers

user markers

re-sync preserving user content

replace-only-managed-region behavior

Phase 2C — Rendered navigation

Build support for:

rendered markdown index files

“when to use this” sections

pack-generated routing text

no required discovery.toml

Phase 2D — Codex adapter framework

Build support for:

external tool adapter concept

Codex as first implementation

generated Codex-facing docs/instructions

Codex commands/skills/install assets pointing back into GROVE

Phase 2E — Base pack slimming

Refactor base so it contains:

minimal universal guidance

anchors

index skeleton

adapter hooks

no heavy opinionated content

Concrete file layout I’d suggest

Something like this:

.grove/
  GROVE.md
  INDEX.md
  rules/
    python.md
    python-testing.md
  docs/
    python-workflows.md
  codex/
    CODEX.md
    commands.md
    skills.md

And inside GROVE.md, keep it lean:

Markdown
# GROVE

## Core Operating Rules
...

## How to Navigate GROVE
See `.grove/INDEX.md` for scoped rules, deep docs, and tool integration files.

## Guidance
<!-- grove:anchor:guidance:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:guidance:end -->

## User Notes
<!-- grove:user:notes:start -->
<!-- grove:user:notes:end -->

That gives you a stable spine.

Important design choice for Codex

Since you said “external tool framework” and only Codex for now, I would make a hard distinction between:

tool adapter assets

pack content assets

Pack content says:

here are the rules

here are the docs

here are the snippets

Adapter assets say:

here is how Codex should consume them

That separation will save you later.

So do not mix:

Python pack content
with

Codex integration logic

Instead let Python pack contribute both:

Python content

optional Codex adapter fragments

But keep them logically separate.

One caution

Since you’re rendering routing into markdown instead of a structured registry, keep the format disciplined.

If every pack writes arbitrary prose into INDEX.md, the index will get muddy.

So I would still impose a schema at render time, even if the output is markdown only.

For example, every pack contributes entries like:

title

applies_when

files

commands

notes

Then the renderer turns that into consistent markdown.

So you are rejecting discovery.toml as a runtime artifact, which makes sense.
But I would still keep structured internal data in the pack manifests so GROVE can render clean markdown indexes.

That gives you human-readable output without losing discipline.

My blunt recommendation

Lock in this mental model:

Phase 2 = composition + preservation + navigation + Codex adapter

Not:

“more templates”

“more packs”

“more docs”

But:

composable documents

safe sync boundaries

human-readable routing

one external adapter done properly

That’s the strong version of Phase 2.

Next, I’d define the exact manifest schema for:

templates

injections

adapters

index_entries

user_regions

And the sync rules for how those are rendered into .grove/.

I can draft that schema next.

the codex integration part isn't quite what i was thinking. we need to do something like knowing what codex or other tools look at so we can inject a hook such as its AGENTS.md that would point to GROVE.md. we can also have something like slash commands pointing at the commands in grove.

Yes — that’s a better framing.

What you’re describing is not really a “Codex integration layer” so much as a tool hook framework:

learn what a tool actually reads

generate the files that tool already honors

make those files hand off to GROVE

optionally generate tool-friendly command entrypoints that map back to GROVE-owned commands

For Codex specifically, the safest hook is AGENTS.md. Codex documents that it reads guidance from AGENTS.md files in the repo tree, including the repo root and the current working directory, merged top-down. It also has built-in slash commands like /init, /status, /approvals, and /model. 
GitHub
+1

I’d rename this subsystem

Not “Codex adapter.”

Call it something like:

tool hooks

tool shims

external tool hooks

compatibility hooks

That better matches what it actually does.

What Phase 2 should do for Codex

I would scope Codex hooks to three things.

1. Generate a root AGENTS.md shim

This is the main hook.

GROVE should render a repo-root AGENTS.md that is intentionally thin and points Codex at .grove/GROVE.md plus the other GROVE files it should consult.

Conceptually:

Markdown
# Codex Project Instructions

This repository uses GROVE as the canonical instruction system.

Read and follow:
- .grove/GROVE.md
- .grove/INDEX.md

When relevant, also consult:
- .grove/rules/*
- .grove/docs/*
- .grove/codex/*

That gives Codex what it expects, while keeping GROVE as the source of truth.

2. Optionally generate nested AGENTS.md shims

Because Codex also reads AGENTS.md in the current working directory, GROVE could render subdirectory-local shims when useful, such as:

src/AGENTS.md

tests/AGENTS.md

docs/AGENTS.md

Those would point to the relevant scoped GROVE files for that subtree. Codex’s own docs say it merges these top-down, so this maps very nicely onto your layered model. 
GitHub

3. Generate Codex-facing command docs, not a separate rule system

This is the part I’d tighten.

I would not assume Codex supports custom slash commands unless we verify it. I was able to confirm built-in slash commands, but I did not find an official source in this pass confirming user-defined slash commands for Codex. 
GitHub

So for Phase 2, I’d do this instead:

generate .grove/codex/CODEX.md

generate .grove/codex/COMMANDS.md

maybe generate prompt snippets or task recipes

Then your root AGENTS.md shim tells Codex to use those.

That gives you command discoverability without betting the design on an unverified Codex feature.

So the architecture becomes
GROVE-owned canonical files

.grove/GROVE.md

.grove/INDEX.md

.grove/rules/...

.grove/docs/...

.grove/codex/...

Tool hook outputs

AGENTS.md at repo root

optional subtree AGENTS.md files

maybe other tool-specific files later for Cursor, Claude Code, etc.

This is much cleaner than treating Codex as a parallel instruction universe.

How this fits into the pack system

I’d add a new contribution type, something like:

TOML
[[contributes.tool_hooks]]
tool = "codex"
kind = "agents_shim"
target = "AGENTS.md"
source = "hooks/codex/root-agents.md"
order = 100

And maybe:

TOML
[[contributes.tool_hooks]]
tool = "codex"
kind = "agents_shim"
target = "tests/AGENTS.md"
source = "hooks/codex/tests-agents.md"
order = 100

That keeps it explicit:

this is a hook for a tool

this is where it writes

this is what it points to

Important nuance on Codex global files

There seems to be some inconsistency or at least historical confusion around Codex’s global guidance location. One source says ~/.codex/AGENTS.md; a Codex issue says the real personal global guidance path was ~/.codex/instructions.md; other issues report behavior differences in interactive sessions. 
GitHub
+3
GitHub
+3
GitHub
+3

Because of that, I would not make global Codex hooks the backbone of Phase 2.

I would center Phase 2 on repo-local AGENTS.md shims, because that’s the most stable and project-controlled hook point from the sources I found. 
GitHub
+1

Best design principle here

I’d make the framework answer one question:

“What files or conventions does this external tool already honor?”

Then GROVE emits compatible hook files.

So later:

Codex → AGENTS.md

Claude Code → whatever its repo instruction file and command structure expects

Cursor → whatever config/prompts surface it actually reads

others → same pattern

That means your abstraction is not “tool integration.”
It is:

tool → hook points → GROVE handoff

That’s the right long-term model.

Updated Phase 2 wording

I’d rewrite that part of Phase 2 as:

External Tool Hooks

detect or define known hook points for supported tools

generate compatible shim files

route those shims back to GROVE canonical docs

keep tool-specific logic thin

never duplicate the full instruction system outside GROVE

For this phase, only implement:

Codex root AGENTS.md shim

optional scoped AGENTS.md shims

Codex-facing GROVE docs for commands/workflows

That feels much closer to what you’re aiming at.

My recommendation

For Codex in Phase 2, I would lock in:

root AGENTS.md shim generation

optional subtree AGENTS.md shim generation

.grove/codex/CODEX.md

.grove/codex/COMMANDS.md

no dependence on custom slash commands yet

no global ~/.codex/... dependence in the first version

That gives you a strong, real hook into Codex without overreaching on uncertain features.

I can draft the exact tool_hooks manifest schema next.
the hooks would actually be put in the actual codex directories. i know cursor uses a .cursor directory.

Yes — that’s the right abstraction.

What we want is a tool hook framework that knows each tool’s real hook points and renders files into those exact locations.

For Phase 2, that means:

GROVE stays canonical

hook files live where the external tool expects them

those hook files are thin shims that point back to GROVE

GROVE can also render tool-specific command docs or wrappers where the tool expects them

The key distinction

There are really two kinds of hook targets:

1. Tool-owned project directories/files

Example: Cursor’s project rules live in .cursor/rules, and Cursor also supports AGENTS.md as a simpler markdown-based instruction mechanism. 
Cursor Documentation
+1

2. Tool-specific global/home directories

Example: ~/.codex/... or similar global config locations.

For Phase 2, I would strongly separate those.

For Cursor

Your instinct is exactly right.

Cursor has real project-level hook points:

.cursor/rules/*.mdc

AGENTS.md

legacy .cursorrules is still supported but deprecated 
Cursor Documentation
+1

So later, a GROVE hook for Cursor should render into actual Cursor locations, not into some invented .grove/cursor/... place and hope Cursor reads it.

That means GROVE could eventually emit:

.cursor/rules/grove-core.mdc

.cursor/rules/python.mdc

maybe subtree .cursor/rules/...

or an AGENTS.md shim if that is the better compatibility path for a given repo 
Cursor Documentation
+1

For Codex

This is the one place I want to be precise.

The most reliable project-level hook I found for Codex is still repo-tree AGENTS.md, not “put files in a Codex project directory.” The sources I found support Codex reading AGENTS.md in the repository tree, and a reported issue indicates ~/.codex/AGENTS.md is not automatically applied to an unrelated project tree by default. 
GitHub

So for Codex Phase 2, I would not make “actual codex directories” the main mechanism unless we later verify a project-local Codex directory convention that Codex truly reads automatically.

Right now, the safe model is:

root AGENTS.md

optional subtree AGENTS.md

both generated by GROVE

both acting as shims back to .grove/GROVE.md

So the framework should look like this

Not “Codex integration.”

More like:

tool_hooks

Each supported tool declares:

tool name

hook type

actual destination path

rendered content source

whether it is global or project-local

Example shape:

TOML
[[contributes.tool_hooks]]
id = "codex-root-agents"
tool = "codex"
hook_type = "project_instruction"
target = "AGENTS.md"
source = "hooks/codex/root-agents.md"
order = 100

[[contributes.tool_hooks]]
id = "cursor-python-rule"
tool = "cursor"
hook_type = "project_rule"
target = ".cursor/rules/python.mdc"
source = "hooks/cursor/python.mdc"
order = 200

That lets GROVE write directly to the real hook points each tool honors.

The design principle I’d lock in

GROVE owns the truth. Tools get shims in their native locations.

So:

Cursor files go in .cursor/...

Codex hook files go where Codex actually reads them

those files should mostly redirect and summarize, not duplicate the whole GROVE system

For your current Phase 2 scope

Since you said only Codex this phase, I’d define it this way:

Phase 2 Codex hook support

generate root AGENTS.md

optionally generate subtree AGENTS.md

each one points back to .grove/GROVE.md

each one may also point to .grove/INDEX.md and relevant scoped rule docs

do not center Phase 2 on ~/.codex/... yet

That keeps it grounded in the hook point we can actually support confidently today. 
GitHub

For later phases

Then the same framework expands to:

Cursor → .cursor/rules/*.mdc or AGENTS.md depending on strategy 
Cursor Documentation
+1

Claude Code → whatever its actual project hook points are

other tools → same pattern

So yes — the hook files should live in the actual tool directories or files the tool honors.
For Cursor, that definitely means .cursor/rules is a first-class target. 
Cursor Documentation
+1

For Codex, today the most defensible project hook is still AGENTS.md in the repo tree, not a Codex-specific project directory.

The next clean step is to define a tool_hooks manifest schema with:

tool

hook_type

target

scope

applies_when

And then define the first concrete implementation for Codex root and subtree AGENTS.md shims.

sounds good. now lets design the minimal base pack. we definitely need GROVE.md with super basic instructions that will always be there. what else should always be there? lets list out the files that are in the base pack

Good—this is exactly where discipline matters. If we get the base pack wrong, everything else gets messy.

You already made the most important decision:

Base pack = infrastructure, not opinion

So the question becomes:

What must always exist for GROVE to function—even in an empty repo with zero feature packs?

Let’s design that cleanly.

🌱 Minimal Base Pack (v1)

I’m going to separate this into:

Core runtime files (required)

Structural scaffolding (anchors + composition targets)

Human navigation layer (minimal)

Tool hooks (Codex only for Phase 2)

1) Core Runtime Files (REQUIRED)

These are non-negotiable. GROVE cannot function without them.

✅ .grove/GROVE.md

The canonical always-loaded file

This should contain:

identity of GROVE

core operating rules (VERY minimal)

how to navigate GROVE

anchor regions for injection

user-editable region

Structure
Markdown
# GROVE

## What This Is
GROVE is the canonical instruction system for this repository.

## Core Rules
- Prefer clarity over cleverness
- Follow repository conventions
- Do not introduce unnecessary complexity

## How to Use GROVE
- This file contains global rules
- See `.grove/INDEX.md` for scoped rules and deeper guidance

## Guidance
<!-- grove:anchor:guidance:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:guidance:end -->

## Commands
<!-- grove:anchor:commands:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:commands:end -->

## User Notes
<!-- grove:user:notes:start -->
<!-- grove:user:notes:end -->

👉 Keep this brutally small.

2) Structural Scaffolding (Composition Targets)

These exist so packs have places to inject into.

✅ .grove/INDEX.md

Human-readable routing layer

This replaces discovery.toml.

It should start mostly empty, but structured.

Markdown
# GROVE Index

## Rules
<!-- grove:anchor:index:rules:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:index:rules:end -->

## Commands
<!-- grove:anchor:index:commands:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:index:commands:end -->

## Tools
<!-- grove:anchor:index:tools:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:index:tools:end -->

## Docs
<!-- grove:anchor:index:docs:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:index:docs:end -->

👉 Packs populate this.

✅ .grove/rules/README.md

Anchor target for rule aggregation

Even if empty initially.

Markdown
# Rules

Scoped rules for specific areas of the repository.

<!-- grove:anchor:rules:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:rules:end -->

👉 Gives packs a safe default injection point.

✅ .grove/docs/README.md

Anchor target for deep docs

Markdown
# Documentation

Detailed reference materials.

<!-- grove:anchor:docs:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:docs:end -->
✅ .grove/commands/README.md

Canonical command surface

Markdown
# Commands

Canonical commands for working in this repository.

<!-- grove:anchor:commands:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:commands:end -->
3) System Metadata (Optional but HIGHLY Recommended)

These are not strictly required, but I would include them in base.

✅ .grove/manifest.toml

Defines the active system state.

TOML
[grove]
version = "0.1.0"

[base_pack]
name = "base"
version = "0.1.0"

Later this becomes:

active packs

versions

sync metadata

👉 This becomes your system brain.

✅ .grove/.gitignore

Protects generated/managed files if needed later.

Minimal for now.

4) Tool Hooks (Codex – Phase 2)
✅ AGENTS.md (repo root)

This is your Codex entry point.

Minimal shim:

Markdown
# Project Instructions

This project uses GROVE as the canonical instruction system.

Read and follow:
- .grove/GROVE.md
- .grove/INDEX.md

When relevant:
- .grove/rules/*
- .grove/docs/*
- .grove/commands/*

👉 That’s it. No duplication.

(Optional) Subtree scaffolding (NOT required in base)

Do not generate:

src/AGENTS.md

tests/AGENTS.md

in the base pack.

Those should be added later by packs or analysis.

📦 Final Base Pack File List

Here is the clean minimal set:

.grove/
  GROVE.md
  INDEX.md

  rules/
    README.md

  docs/
    README.md

  commands/
    README.md

  manifest.toml

AGENTS.md
❌ What should NOT be in base

This is just as important.

Do NOT include:

language rules (Python, JS, etc.)

testing frameworks

linting configs

workflow opinions

CI/CD instructions

long documentation

tool-specific complexity (beyond Codex shim)

👉 All of that belongs in packs.

🔥 Design Philosophy (lock this in)

Base pack should:

define structure

define boundaries

define injection points

define navigation

define identity

Base pack should NOT:

define behavior for specific domains

define heavy rules

define workflows beyond universal basics

💡 One subtle but important addition

I strongly recommend one more thing in base:

Add a “commands anchor” to GROVE.md

Because commands are one of the highest-value injections.

You already saw it, but I want to emphasize it:

Markdown
## Commands
<!-- grove:anchor:commands:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:commands:end -->

This becomes:

where packs inject canonical commands

where Codex (and later tools) can quickly find them

🚀 My blunt recommendation

If you build only ONE thing correctly in Phase 2:

Build the base pack + anchor system + safe sync.

Everything else (packs, tools, commands, discovery) becomes easy after that.

If you want next step, I’d do:

👉 define the exact pack.toml schema for:

templates

injections

tool_hooks

index contributions

That’s the next critical piece.
what about explaining the GROVE acronym and base philosophy or is that not needed for the agent?

Short answer: yes, but keep it extremely tight and functional.

You don’t want GROVE.md to turn into a manifesto… but you do want just enough philosophy so every agent behaves consistently without guessing.

🎯 The real question

You’re not asking:

“Should we explain GROVE?”

You’re asking:

“What minimum shared mental model does every agent need to operate correctly?”

That is worth encoding.

🧠 What the agent actually needs

From everything you’ve built so far, the agent needs to internalize exactly three things:

1. GROVE is the source of truth

Not the agent’s memory. Not vibes. Not guessing.

2. Context is layered (load only what’s needed)

This is the WISC idea baked into GROVE.

3. Structure > improvisation

Follow files, rules, commands, and navigation.

🌱 So yes — include the acronym

But not as branding fluff.

Use it as a behavioral compression mechanism.

This is actually powerful because it encodes the system in 5 letters.

✨ Recommended GROVE section (minimal + sharp)

Put this near the top of GROVE.md.

Markdown
## GROVE Principles

GROVE defines how this system operates.

- **G — Grow knowledge**  
  Persist useful information in the system, not just in memory.

- **R — Root tasks**  
  Break work into clear, structured steps.

- **O — Optimize context**  
  Load only what is needed. Do not assume or overfetch.

- **V — Verify progress**  
  Check work against rules, commands, and expected outcomes.

- **E — Enrich the ecosystem**  
  Feed improvements back into GROVE (rules, docs, commands).

That’s it.

No paragraphs. No storytelling.

🔥 Add ONE critical line under it

This is the most important sentence in your whole system:

Markdown
Agents do not hold knowledge — the grove does.

That anchors everything.

🧭 Add a tiny operational philosophy section

Right after GROVE principles, add this:

Markdown
## Operating Model

- GROVE is the canonical source of instructions.
- Use `.grove/INDEX.md` to find relevant rules and docs.
- Follow scoped rules before making assumptions.
- Prefer existing commands and workflows over inventing new ones.
🚫 What NOT to include

Do NOT include:

long explanations of why GROVE exists

ecosystem metaphors beyond the acronym

philosophical essays

“inspiration” language

Why?

Because:

👉 Tokens matter
👉 Agents skim
👉 Signal > story

🧠 Why this matters more than it seems

Without this section:

agents drift into improvisation

agents over-rely on prior knowledge

agents ignore the layered system

agents don’t feed improvements back

With this section:

behavior becomes consistent across all agents

packs plug into a stable mental model

tool hooks (Codex, later Cursor) behave predictably

💡 Subtle design win

Your acronym is doing something clever:

It encodes:

memory system (G)

planning system (R)

context system (O)

validation system (V)

feedback loop (E)

That’s basically an agent operating system in 5 letters.

So yeah — keep it.

🧩 Final answer

Include:

GROVE acronym (tight, bullet-based)

one-line core principle (“Agents do not hold knowledge…”)

minimal operating model (4–5 bullets)

That’s all.

If you want next, I can:

👉 rewrite your entire GROVE.md base file to be perfectly minimal, high-signal, and Phase 2-ready (with anchors, philosophy, and zero fluff)

what about having a system baked into the grove that allows agents to write back into the grove for memory and self improvement, even having a command to help upgrade itself? maybe that should be a memory pack and a self upgrade pack?

Yes — I would not bake that directly into the minimal base pack.

I think it should exist in GROVE as a first-class concept, but as optional packs, not base behavior.

Why I would not put it in base

The base pack should answer:

what GROVE is

how agents should navigate it

how composition and sync work

what universal rules always apply

As soon as base starts owning:

memory writeback

self-modification workflows

upgrade routines

agent reflection loops

it stops being minimal infrastructure and starts becoming an opinionated operating model.

That is too much for base.

My recommendation

Split this into at least two packs, exactly like you suggested:

1. Memory pack

This pack handles:

persistent memory files

writeback conventions

what kinds of facts/decisions can be saved

where memories go

how to avoid junk-drawer memory

commands for adding/updating memory

This is about remembering.

2. Self-upgrade pack

This pack handles:

proposing improvements to GROVE

writing candidate updates

upgrade workflow

review/approval rules

commands for improving rules, docs, commands, or hooks

This is about changing the system.

That separation is clean and important.

Memory is about:

project facts

preferences

decisions

current status

Self-upgrade is about:

the instruction system itself

pack quality

new commands

rule refinements

hook improvements

Those are related, but not the same.

What base should still say

Even if the behavior lives in packs, base should still encode one small principle:

GROVE may be enriched over time through structured updates.

That way the idea is present, but the mechanics are not forced on every repo.

So base can acknowledge the ecosystem grows, without implementing growth machinery itself.

A good mental model

Think of it like this:

base pack = filesystem + constitution

memory pack = notebook

self-upgrade pack = maintenance crew

You want every repo to have the constitution.
You do not necessarily want every repo to have an active notebook and auto-maintenance workflow on day one.

What the memory pack might own

Something like:

.grove/
  memory/
    MEMORY.md
    decisions.md
    preferences.md
    current-status.md

And commands like:

update memory

record decision

save preference

summarize current status

But with rules about:

only store durable value

avoid redundant clutter

prefer structured entries

distinguish facts from temporary chatter

What the self-upgrade pack might own

Something like:

.grove/
  improvement/
    IMPROVEMENT.md
    proposals/

And commands like:

propose grove improvement

refine rule

add command

upgrade hook

review pack gap

With a workflow like:

identify weakness

draft proposal

validate impact

present diff

apply only through explicit approval

That last part matters a lot.

Important caution

I would be very careful about anything that sounds like:

“agent can freely rewrite GROVE”

“agent self-improves automatically”

“agent upgrades its own rules without review”

That is how you get drift, bloat, and instruction rot.

So I would make the self-upgrade pack proposal-driven, not autonomous-overwrite-driven.

Meaning:

agents can propose

maybe agents can draft

maybe agents can stage changes

but application should be deliberate

My strongest recommendation

Use three layers:

Base pack

Includes only:

philosophy

structure

navigation

anchors

tool hook scaffolding

Memory pack

Optional, handles:

persistent repo memory

decisions

preferences

current status

writeback commands

Self-upgrade pack

Optional, handles:

improvement proposals

rule refinement workflows

command additions

hook updates

review/apply process

That is the clean architecture.

One subtle thing I do think base should include

I would include a tiny anchor for future improvement notes, but not the full system.

For example in GROVE.md:

Markdown
## Improvement
<!-- grove:anchor:improvement:start -->
<!-- grove:anchor:improvement:end -->

That gives the self-upgrade pack a place to inject a short policy later, without forcing it into base now.

Final answer

Yes, GROVE should support memory and self-improvement.

No, I would not bake the full mechanism into the minimal base pack.

I would make:

a memory pack

a self-upgrade pack

And I would keep self-upgrade proposal-based and reviewable, not freeform self-rewriting.

The cleanest next step is to define the base pack file list first, then define the first optional packs right after it:

base

memory

self-upgrade

Oh I like where this is going. Now we’re moving from “framework” to something you can actually use day one without it feeling empty.

And yes — I agree with your instincts. The packs you’re suggesting are exactly the right kind: high-leverage, low-opinion, broadly useful.

Let’s structure this cleanly so Phase 2 gives you a working system, not just scaffolding.

🌱 Phase 2 Pack Set (Practical + High ROI)

I’m going to group these into tiers so we don’t accidentally overbuild.

🧩 Tier 1 — Core Usability Packs (Build These First)

These make GROVE immediately useful in any repo.

✅ 1. Memory Pack

You already called this — absolutely essential.

Purpose

persistent, structured project memory

decisions, preferences, current state

Why it matters
Without this, agents forget everything between sessions.

Owns

.grove/memory/
  MEMORY.md
  decisions.md
  preferences.md
  current-status.md

Commands

record decision

update memory

summarize state

✅ 2. Python Pack

Also a must-have for you specifically.

Purpose

coding standards

testing norms

common commands

structure expectations

Owns

.grove/rules/python.md
.grove/rules/python-testing.md
.grove/docs/python-workflows.md

Injects into

GROVE.md (guidance + commands)

INDEX.md (rules + commands)

✅ 3. Commands Pack (Core Dev Commands)

This is subtle but very important.

Purpose
Define canonical commands so agents don’t guess.

Owns

.grove/commands/dev.md

Examples

install deps

run tests

lint

format

run app

👉 This becomes the source of truth for execution

🧠 Tier 2 — Intelligence & Context Packs

These make agents smarter, not just structured.

✅ 4. Knowledge Pack (Your idea — excellent)

I strongly agree with this.

This is actually one of the most powerful packs you can build.

Purpose

curated knowledge of libraries/tools

links to repos

best practices

internal summaries

Owns

.grove/knowledge/
  python/
    fastapi.md
    pytorch.md
  tools/
    langchain.md
    uv.md

What goes inside

repo link

when to use

gotchas

recommended patterns

👉 Think of this as your personal “engineering playbook”

✅ 5. Project Context Pack

This complements memory but is more structured.

Purpose

define what the project is

goals, scope, constraints

Owns

.grove/project/
  goals.md
  scope.md
  architecture.md

Why this matters
Memory is incremental.
This is intentional grounding.

⚙️ Tier 3 — System Evolution Packs

These are powerful but should stay optional.

✅ 6. Self-Upgrade Pack

We talked about this — keep it proposal-driven.

Purpose

improve GROVE itself

refine rules, commands, structure

Owns

.grove/improvement/
  proposals/
  IMPROVEMENT.md

Commands

propose improvement

refine rule

add command

✅ 7. Task / Planning Pack

This one is huge for real workflows.

Purpose

structured task execution

planning + tracking

Owns

.grove/tasks/
  TASKS.md
  current.md

Commands

create task

update task

mark complete

plan work

👉 This prevents agents from being reactive blobs.

🧪 Tier 4 — Nice-to-Have (But High Potential)

Not required for Phase 2, but worth listing.

🔹 Testing Pack

deeper testing strategies

coverage expectations

🔹 Documentation Pack

how to write docs

structure for README / API docs

🔹 Research Pack (for your PhD work 👀)

paper notes

citations

experiment logs

🔹 Multi-Agent Coordination Pack

how agents communicate

roles and handoffs

🧠 My Opinion on Your Suggested Packs
Memory pack → 100% yes

Core system capability.

Python pack → 100% yes

You’ll use this constantly.

Knowledge pack → this is a power move

This is where your system becomes better than generic AI tools.

Most people don’t build this. You should.

🔥 The 6 Packs I Would Actually Build in Phase 2

If we keep this tight and high-impact:

MUST BUILD

base (you already are)

memory

python

commands

STRONGLY RECOMMENDED

knowledge

project-context

OPTIONAL (but valuable)

self-upgrade

tasks

🧩 How They Work Together (Important Insight)

This is the real magic:

base → structure + philosophy

memory → what has happened

project-context → what we’re trying to do

python → how to implement

commands → how to execute

knowledge → how to do it well

That’s basically a full operating system.

⚠️ One important constraint

Don’t let packs become:

giant documentation dumps

random notes

unstructured text

Every pack should:

inject into INDEX

have clear “when to use this”

have small, focused files

💡 One extra idea (you’ll like this)

For the knowledge pack, add this pattern:

Markdown
## When to Use
## When NOT to Use
## Common Pitfalls
## Recommended Pattern
## Example

That structure alone will massively improve agent output quality.

🚀 Final Answer

Yes — I agree with your packs, and I’d formalize Phase 2 around:

Core Packs

base

memory

python

commands

Intelligence Packs

knowledge

project-context

Optional Evolution Packs

self-upgrade

tasks

If you want next, I can:

👉 design the pack.toml schema for these packs
👉 or fully spec one pack (memory or knowledge) end-to-end so you can implement immediately

one other question is how to teach it to use subagents when possible. maybe that is in some sort of planning/execution pack where it tells what items are dependent on what and if there are multiple tasks that can be done in parallel, it is told to spin up multiple subagents?

Yes to both.

Commands pack

I think you’re seeing the right shape:

commands should be structured

GROVE should render human-readable command docs

agents should not have to infer commands from prose

So I would absolutely make commands originate from structured data, then render into markdown.

Best model

Have a project-owned command registry, probably TOML, that GROVE reads and renders into:

.grove/commands/*.md

injected command summaries in .grove/GROVE.md

command listings in .grove/INDEX.md

Something like this
TOML
# .grove/commands.toml

[[commands]]
id = "test"
title = "Run tests"
command = "uv run pytest"
when_to_use = "Use when validating Python changes."
category = "validation"

[[commands]]
id = "lint"
title = "Run lint"
command = "uv run ruff check ."
when_to_use = "Use before finalizing code changes."
category = "quality"

[[commands]]
id = "format"
title = "Format code"
command = "uv run ruff format ."
when_to_use = "Use to normalize Python formatting."
category = "quality"

Then GROVE renders that into markdown like:

Markdown
# Commands

## Validation
- **Run tests** — `uv run pytest`
  - Use when validating Python changes.

## Quality
- **Run lint** — `uv run ruff check .`
  - Use before finalizing code changes.

That is much better than hand-writing commands in random docs.

Where this file should live

I’d make it part of the commands pack, but project-owned once installed.

Probably one of these:

.grove/commands.toml

.grove/config/commands.toml

I’d lean toward:

.grove/commands.toml

Simple and obvious.

Why TOML is a good fit

human-editable

structured

easy to validate

easy to render

consistent with pack manifests

So yes: commands should come from TOML and be rendered into the GROVE markdown files.

Planning / execution / subagent usage

Yes again — but I would not hide this inside the commands pack.

This should be its own pack, something like:

planning pack

execution pack

or combined planning-execution pack

My preference is planning-execution pack for Phase 2, because the two ideas are tightly linked.

What this pack should teach

It should encode things like:

break work into tasks

identify dependencies

identify independent workstreams

use subagents when tasks can be parallelized or specialized

avoid spawning subagents for tiny or tightly coupled work

merge results carefully

verify before final integration

That is exactly the kind of behavior that should be taught explicitly.

The key idea

You do not want a vague rule like:

“Use subagents when possible.”

That will create chaos.

You want something more like:

Use subagents when work can be decomposed into independent or weakly coupled tasks, especially when specialized analysis or parallel execution will reduce time or improve quality.

That gives the agent a real decision rule.

I would define this pack around four concepts
1. Task decomposition

Teach the agent to split work into:

atomic tasks

dependencies

outputs

validation criteria

2. Parallelism

Teach the agent to identify:

which tasks block others

which tasks can run independently

which tasks are too coupled to parallelize safely

3. Subagent delegation

Teach the agent when to spawn subagents:

specialized research

code inspection in separate areas

parallel implementation candidates

independent document drafting

test planning while code changes are happening

And when not to:

tiny tasks

tasks requiring shared mutable context

tasks where merge cost exceeds benefit

4. Reconciliation

Teach the parent agent to:

collect outputs

compare against plan

resolve conflicts

run final validation

update memory/tasks if needed

The structure I’d use

Something like:

.grove/
  planning/
    PLANNING.md
    subagents.md
    dependency-patterns.md

And maybe structured config too, such as:

TOML
# .grove/planning.toml

[subagents]
encouraged = true
max_parallel_tasks = 3

[[delegation_patterns]]
id = "independent_file_analysis"
when = "Multiple files or directories can be analyzed independently."
delegate = true

[[delegation_patterns]]
id = "shared_refactor"
when = "Changes require tight coordination across shared interfaces."
delegate = false

Then render this into markdown the same way commands are rendered.

My advice on the wording

You should teach the system to think in terms of:

dependency graph

parallelizable work

specialized delegation

merge and verify

That is stronger than just “subagents.”

So the planning-execution pack might include rules like:

Delegation heuristic

Spawn subagents when:

tasks are independent

tasks benefit from specialization

tasks can be validated separately

merging outputs is straightforward

Do not spawn subagents when:

work is highly coupled

context is unstable

the task is too small

coordination overhead outweighs benefit

That’s the real operating logic.

How these two packs fit together

This is actually a nice pairing:

Commands pack

Answers:

what commands exist

when to run them

what validation commands are canonical

Planning-execution pack

Answers:

how to break work down

when to delegate

when to parallelize

when to validate

how to reconcile results

Together they give you:

execution mechanics

orchestration mechanics

That’s very strong.

My recommendation for Phase 2 pack list now

I’d upgrade the earlier list to:

Core

base

memory

commands

project-context

Practical implementation

python

knowledge

Agent operating behavior

planning-execution

Optional later

self-upgrade

testing

documentation

research

multi-agent coordination refinement

If I were prioritizing actual build order, I’d probably do:

base

commands

memory

planning-execution

python

project-context

knowledge

That order gives you a usable operating system very quickly.

My blunt take

Yes, commands should be structured in TOML and rendered into GROVE markdown.

And yes, subagent usage should be taught through a dedicated planning-execution pack that explicitly models dependency-aware delegation and parallel work, not through a vague one-liner in base.

The clean next step is to define two schemas:

commands.toml

planning.toml

And decide which parts are project-owned versus pack-owned.

some of these seem more like agent skills than just plain md files. how could we incorporate agent skills into this? look online for how codex works with skills to help us out. should we put the skills in the codex directories and refer to them? or can we have them in the grove? im not sure codex would know to look in the grove

This is a really important question—and you’re absolutely right to pause here. You’re hitting the boundary between:

Context files (GROVE, rules, docs)

Agent skills (procedural capabilities)

These are not the same thing, and Codex treats them differently.

Let me break this down cleanly so you can design GROVE correctly.

🧠 What Codex “skills” actually are

Codex skills are not just markdown files.

They are:

modular folders that package instructions, workflows, and optional scripts 
Agent Skills

Each skill has:

skill-name/
  SKILL.md        ← required (name + description + instructions)
  references/     ← optional deeper docs
  scripts/        ← optional executable logic
🔑 Critical behavior

Codex does NOT load the full skill automatically

It only sees:

skill name

description

Then decides whether to use it

THEN loads the body if needed 
Agent Skills

👉 This is very similar to your GROVE layered model.

⚠️ Where Codex looks for skills

This is the key constraint:

Codex discovers skills from its own skill directories, typically:

~/.codex/skills/... 
GitHub

Skills are installed via tools like $skill-installer

They are not automatically discovered inside .grove/

👉 So your intuition is correct:

Codex will NOT naturally look inside .grove for skills.

🧩 So how do we integrate GROVE + Skills?

This is the architecture decision:

❌ Option 1 — Put skills only in .grove

Bad idea.

Codex won’t discover them

You lose automatic triggering

You’d have to rely on AGENTS.md hacks

❌ Option 2 — Put everything in Codex skill folders

Also bad.

GROVE loses control

duplication

fragmentation of system

✅ Option 3 (Correct): Dual-layer architecture

GROVE = source of truth
Codex Skills = executable capability layer

🏗️ Recommended Architecture
Layer 1 — GROVE (your system)

Lives in:

.grove/

Owns:

rules

commands

knowledge

planning logic

philosophy

👉 This is the brain

Layer 2 — Codex Skills (external execution layer)

Lives in:

~/.codex/skills/

or installed via repo-based installer.

Owns:

workflows

procedures

automation

subagent orchestration

repeatable tasks

👉 This is the hands

🔥 The key insight

Your packs like:

planning-execution

commands

knowledge

👉 These are actually skill definitions in disguise

But:

GROVE = describes them

Skills = execute them

🧠 How they should connect
GROVE → Skills (reference layer)

In .grove/planning/subagents.md:

Markdown
## Subagent Delegation

Use the `parallel-execution` skill when:
- tasks are independent
- work can be parallelized

See Codex skill: `parallel-execution`
Skills → GROVE (context layer)

Inside a skill’s SKILL.md:

Markdown
# Parallel Execution Skill

## Description
Execute independent tasks in parallel using subagents.

## Instructions
Before execution:
- Read `.grove/GROVE.md`
- Read `.grove/planning/PLANNING.md`

Then:
1. Identify independent tasks
2. Spawn subagents
3. Merge results
4. Validate using `.grove/commands`
🎯 This gives you:

GROVE = what should be done

Skills = how to do it procedurally

That separation is gold.

🧠 Where your packs map to skills

Here’s the important realization:

GROVE Pack	Skill Needed?	Why
memory	❌ maybe	mostly passive storage
commands	⚠️ optional	execution helpers
python	❌	rules only
knowledge	❌	reference only
planning-execution	✅ YES	procedural
self-upgrade	✅ YES	procedural
💥 The big takeaway

Not all packs become skills.

Only packs that involve:

multi-step workflows

decision logic

delegation

automation

👉 should become skills.

🧩 Where should skills live?

For Phase 2:

Recommended approach
1. Keep skills in the repo (source of truth)
.grove/skills/
  planning-execution/
    SKILL.md
    references/

👉 This is YOUR version-controlled definition

2. Install them into Codex

Via:

$skill-installer

or a future GROVE command like:

Bash
grove install-skills

Which copies/syncs to:

~/.codex/skills/
3. Use AGENTS.md as the bridge
Markdown
# AGENTS.md

This repo uses GROVE + Codex Skills.

Relevant skills:
- planning-execution
- code-refactor
- test-validation

See `.grove/GROVE.md` for rules and context.
🧠 Why this design is powerful

Because it aligns perfectly with how Codex actually works:

skills = modular capability bundles 
GitHub

skills trigger based on description

skills load context lazily

skills can include scripts + workflows

And it keeps GROVE clean and central.

⚠️ One subtle but critical rule

Do NOT duplicate logic.

Bad:

GROVE says how to plan

Skill also redefines planning

Good:

GROVE defines planning rules

Skill executes planning process

🚀 Final Answer

Yes — some of your packs are actually skills in disguise

Codex will NOT discover skills inside .grove automatically

You should:

✅ Keep skills defined in GROVE (repo-controlled)
✅ Install them into Codex skill directories
✅ Use AGENTS.md as the bridge
✅ Keep GROVE as the source of truth
🔥 My recommendation (very concrete)

For Phase 2:

Build these 2 skills first:

planning-execution skill

task breakdown

subagent spawning

parallelization

reconciliation

self-upgrade skill

propose improvements

refine rules

update GROVE

Everything else stays as GROVE packs.

If you want next step, I can:

👉 design your first real SKILL.md (planning-execution)
👉 or design the grove install-skills command + sync model
